speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This is tape eight. Today is December 22nd, 1995. We are here with Nuremberg court reporter, Vivien Spitz, at her home in Aurora, Colorado, United States of America. I am Gary [? LuBelle, ?] and we are-- we have conducted this interview in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just go ahead and read from that slate right  there.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- in this photograph, the man on the left is Grand Admiral Karl Doenitz of the German Navy and U-boat fame. Commander of the German Navy. He has already been tried in the major Nazi trial-- Goering's trial-- and sentenced to 10 years in prison. The other two men-- one leaning forward at Doenitz's face, and the other man at the end of the table are interpreters. The man sitting next to me, to my right, is Judge Michael A. Musmanno of Pennsylvania, who wanted to take Doenitz out of prison for a-- an interrogation about the cooperation and the working together of the German Navy and the Italian Navy. Doenitz appears here in his prison suit, or garb, coming directly from prison. And Judge Musmanno chose not to wear his black judicial robe for the purpose of this interrogation, but his American Naval officer's uniform. At this point-- yes, and-- and I am the court reporter, Vivien Spitz, to the extreme right. This was taken in Nuremberg in-- I'm sure it was 1947. And I have a carbon copy of this interrogation that I took. Very definitely, because there was a date.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you go ahead, and tell us what this is, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an original carbon copy of the statement that was taken of Grand Admiral Karl Doenitz commander of the German Navy and U-boat commander, by Judge Michael Amos [? Manno ?] of Pennsylvania, in a post-trial interrogation, after Doenitz had been sentenced to 10 years in prison. This particular copy shows my transcript. And then the interpreter's corrections of his own-- maybe not errors, but he found better words for interpretation than originally I reported.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You can read from that slate right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of Dachau with the moat. And this was provided by a war correspondence after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Country, date?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dachau, Germany, just after liberation in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of a discovery upon liberation at Dachau, Germany, some time after the war ended in 1945, provided by a war correspondent.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph of the camp Ebensee in Austria, after liberation in 1945, provided by a war correspondent.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an award, an honor that I received, on December 2, 1995, from the America Israel Friendship League for the presentations that I make to rebut the claims that the Holocaust never happened. And I--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is an award and an honor that I received from the Beth Joseph Congregation in Denver, Colorado, a humanitarian award given annually for, in my case, for the presentations that I make to rebut the claims that the Holocaust never happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Glass of water. What have I got here? I got water here. Let me see what I can get.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, you ready? OK. Go Ooh. Ouch. [LAUGHTER] Sorry.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- an aerial shot of the Palace of Justice at Nuremberg, Germany, in which all 13 trials were held, including the Nuremberg prison complex. The photograph was taken, I believe, in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the first of four shots that I took when I first arrived in Nuremberg of the devastated, bombed out, walled city. 1946, November. This is the second shot that I took of the bombed out, walled city in Nuremberg, Germany, in November, 1946. This is the third shot of the bombed out, walled city of Nuremberg, Germany that I took in November, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the final shot that I took of the bombed out, walled city in Nuremberg, Germany in November, 1946. This is a pre-war shot of the Grand Hotel in Nuremberg, Germany, right across the street from the walled city. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I do not know the year this was taken, because it was before the bombing.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now in this one, I think-- who are the key? Well you're here, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to describe where you are.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the justices--

speaker: interviewee
text: Those are the judges. And the chief judge is Walter Beals, second from the left. And I-- I wish I had gotten the names, [INTERPOSING VOICES] Gary, of the judges. Do you want me to go upstairs?

speaker: interviewer
text: Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a scene, an Associated Press photograph of the courtroom in Nuremberg, Germany. This is the main courtroom. The four judges on the bench. The second judge from the left is the Chief Judge Walter Beals of Seattle. And the other judges, two of them are Judge Sebring and Judge Swearingen. And I do not remember the name of the fourth judge. General Telford Taylor at the lectern-- the prosecutor. I am the court reporter on the lower dais to the extreme left with my head in my hand. This photograph was taken in 1947.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we're rolling. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a shot of the medical case in Nuremberg, Germany, in 1947. Karl Brandt in the number one seat in the front row to the extreme left-- Hitler's personal physician. Then Siegfried. [POINTS] And then next to him, [POINTS] Siegfried Handloser-- H-A-N-D-L-O-S-E-R. Next to him, Paul Rostock-- R-O-S-T-O-C-K. From left to right, I'm identifying. Oskar Schroeder, [POINTS] Karl Genzken-- G-E-N-Z-K-E-N, Karl Gebhardt, Kurt Blome-- B-L-O-M-E, Joachim Mrugowksy-- M-R-U-G-O-W-S-K-Y, Rudolf Brandt, Helmut Poppendick, [COUGHING] Wolfram Sievers. The back row-- Gerhard Rose, Siegfried Ruff-- R-U-F-F, Viktor Brack, Hans-Wolfgang Romberg, Hermann Becker-Freyseng-- B-E-C-K-E-R hyphen F-R-E-Y-S-E-N-G, Georg August Weltz, Konrad Schafer, Waldemar Hoven, Wilhelm Beiglbock, Adolf Pokorny, Herta Oberheuser, Fritz Fischer. This is the first shot of the high altitude experiment, captured German film, Germany. And I do not know the year. It would have been 1942 to '43. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] '43, That's right. This is the second shot in sequence of the high altitude experiment-- the first experiment at Dachau, 1942 to 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the third shot-- not yet? This is the third shot of the high altitude sequential frame, 1942 to 1943, Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gary. This is the fourth sequential shot of the high altitude experiment at Dachau, to the point of death, 1942 to 1943. This is the survivor of the bone, muscle, and nerve regeneration and transplantation experiment, Carol Luska, in the courtroom at Nuremberg, Germany, 1947. This is a captured German film, showing the experimental subject's leg, Carol Luska, who testified. This is a captured film, as I said. This is captured German film of another victim, Maria Kusmierczyk at Ravensbrueck concentration camp. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I do not know the date. Let me see. Bone, muscle, and nerve, 1941 to 1945. This is the bone, muscle, and nerve regeneration and transplantation experiment from 1941 to 1945. A bin of legs. [CLEARING THROAT] This was at Ravensbrueck concentration camp in Germany. This is another captured German photograph of the bone, muscle, and nerve regeneration and transplantation experiment at Ravensbrueck Germany, from 1941 to 1945. This is a shot of one of the 112 Jews selected for the skeleton collection at the University of Strasbourg under Nazi occupation. This is a shot of the phosphorus burns in the Buchenwald concentration camp 19-- from 1943 to 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Look at what we've got. We got the names.

speaker: interviewer
text: There we go. See? Sebring, Beals--

speaker: interviewee
text: Beals. Who's that other one?

speaker: interviewer
text: Johnson--

speaker: interviewee
text: Crawford.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Swatson--

speaker: interviewee
text: Swearingen. Crawford. Ah, do you need?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a shot of the four judges-- American judges-- who tried the medical case in Nuremberg, Germany-- Judges Sebring, Beals-- who was the presiding judge-- Crawford, and Swearingen-- who was the alternate judge.

speaker: interviewer
text: Upstairs. Or did we not shoot that? No, we shot that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah we did. This is a shot of the ovens at Dachau. I do not know the year the photograph was taken. Provided to me by the Army Signal Corps photographer upon liberation. This is another shot of the ovens. And this was provided by the US Army Signal Corps photographer who liberated Dachau with General Felix Sparks in 1945. This is another shot provided by the US Army Signal Corps photographer with General Sparks, of, upon the liberation at Dachau, of the ovens. This is a shot of a pile of the corpses awaiting the crematoria, provided by the US Army Signal Corps photographer with General Felix Sparks upon the liberation of Dachau. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The photograph was taken in [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] May-- or on April 1945. This is a shot of the piled up corpses awaiting the crematoria, provided by the US Signal Corps photographer at the liberation of Buchenwald. Taken after liberation. I do not know the date.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. This was provided by the Army Signal Corps photographer upon the liberation of Dachau with General Sparks in April, 1945. This is a box car of half-dead victims for the concentration camp, provided by the Army Signal Corps photographer who liberated Dachau in April, 1945.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a shot provided by the US Army Signal Corps photographer upon liberation of Buchenwald concentration camp in 1945. [CLEARING THROAT]

speaker: interviewer
text: I think this is the last one. Last one.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a shot of heil Hitler in his favorite pose. I mean, I'm sorry, of Adolf Hitler in his favorite pose in Germany. And I do not know the year the photograph was taken. Trying to identity.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Go.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a shot of President Jimmy Carter's State of the Union address on national television within two months after he established the President's Commission on the Holocaust in 1978. This would have been January of 1979.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Washing--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell who these two people are in the middle.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was in Washington, DC, of course, in the House of Representatives. And this is Vice President Walter Mondale and Speaker Thomas P O'Neill. In the top chair. President Jimmy Carter reaching over to acknowledge me, Vivien Spitz, as the chief reporter of debates.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well that's a wrap, as they say in showbiz.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a wrap.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's a wrap.
